request.question: Who besides Marceau founded Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program that was featured in a 2005 American documentary?

title: 100 Classrooms program
Take the Lead
Yvonne Marceau
One World Youth Project
Potisarn Pittayakorn School
Pierre Dulaine
Mad Hot Ballroom
Index of Afghanistan-related articles
Theodore C. Marceau
2010 Panasonic Gobel Awards
sentences: The 100 Classrooms program was a joint project by Deutsche Welle and Cap Anamur to build and restore classrooms in Afghanistan after the U.S. invasion of 2001.
Beginning June 13, 2003, the program helped to build nearly 300 classrooms and 32 schools.
Take the Lead is a 2006 drama dance film directed by Liz Friedlander, and starring Antonio Banderas as dance instructor Pierre Dulaine, the founder of Dancing Classrooms.
It also stars Alfre Woodard, John Ortiz, Rob Brown, Yaya DaCosta, Dante Basco, Elijah Kelley, and Jenna Dewan.
The film was released on April 7, 2006.
Although based in New York City, it was filmed in Toronto.
Stock footage of various locations in New York City were used.
Yvonne Marceau is an American ballroom dancer, choreographer, and instructor.
She was born in Chicago, IL, and holds a B.F.A. from the University of Utah as well as an associate degree from the Imperial Society of Ballroom Dance.
In 1984, Marceau co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine.
Together, Marceau and Dulaine won numerous ballroom dance contests and were four-time winners of the British Exhibition ballroom competition.
Marceau is the recipient of various awards, including the Astaire Award and the Dance Magazine Award.
Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom.

Marceau has been on the faculty at The Juilliard School since 1993 and has taught at the School of American Ballet as well as for numerous New York City social groups, including the Union Club and cotillion societies.
Marceau also currently teaches at the NYU Tisch New Studio on Broadway.
One World Youth Project (OWYP) is a non-profit 501(c)(3) corporation founded in Massachusetts and currently based in Washington DC.
The goal of One World Youth Project is to enhance education towards a more discerning, empathetic and empowered generation of global citizens.

In 2009, One World Youth Project launched the One World Hub program, a service-learning program that trains university students to lead a global education curriculum in local middle/high schools, and to connect these secondary school classrooms with partner classrooms in other countries.
The One World Youth Project global education curriculum includes the following units: cultural exchange, understanding of global challenges, community mapping, and service-learning.
Potisarn Pittayakorn is a coeducational school for grades 7 to 12 in Thailand.
Founded by Nuan Thamutaro (นวล ธมมธโร) in 1956, Potisarn is built on Mr. and Mrs. Saiputhong's land in an area of 6 rai 440 square wah.
When the school started in 1956, there was no classroom or any buildings.
Students had to study in a temple.
Later, Nuan Thamutaro raised funds for a school building.
The result was a 2-floor wooden building that contained 12 classrooms.
This building was finished in 1958.
In 1982, Potisarn School got funded for a 3-floor concrete building that contained 18 classrooms.
In 1983, the pool and toilet were built.
Later in 1984, the elementary education department selected Potisarn School into its elementary improvement program.
In this program, Potisarn's students got free books, free uniforms and free shelter.
In 1986, Suankularb Wittayalai recruited Potisarn to be their sub-school.
That meant any Potisarn students who met the requirements would be eligible to get into Suankalarb Wittayalai without any further exam.
Now the school consists of 3 main buildings, 1 medical building and 1 football field.
Pierre Dulaine (born 23 April 1944) is a dance instructor and dancer.
He invented the Dulaine method of teaching dance.
He also founded Dancing Classrooms, a Social and Emotional development program for 5th grade children that uses ballroom dancing as a vehicle to change the lives of the children and their families.

Mad Hot Ballroom is a 2005 American documentary film directed and co-produced by Marilyn Agrelo and written and co-produced by Amy Sewell, about a ballroom dance program in the New York City Department of Education, the New York City public school system for fifth graders.
Several styles of dance are shown in the film, such as tango, foxtrot, swing, rumba and merengue.
– 100 Classrooms program
Theodore Christopher Marceau (May 28, 1859 - June 22, 1922) was an American photographer.
He pioneered the creation of a national chain of photographic studios in the United States in the 1880s.
He founded the "Marceau Studios" in Manhattan, New York City, Philadelphia and Boston and was one of the most widely known photographers in the country.

The 13th Annual Panasonic Gobel Awards (or the 13th Annual Panasonic Awards) honoring the favorite in television programming/production works/individual, was held on March 26, 2010, at the Ballroom Theater Djakarta XXI in Jalan M.H. Thamrin, Menteng, Central Jakarta.
RCTI, Global TV and RCTI televised the ceremony in the Indonesia.
This 2010 ceremony awards this issue titled "Indonesia Unite".
The event coincides with the Golden Year of PT Panasonic Gobel Indonesia, as the organizer of Panasonic Awards.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Who besides Marceau founded Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program that was featured in a 2005 American documentary?
hop1_docs: Yvonne Marceau | Yvonne Marceau is an American ballroom dancer, choreographer, and instructor.  She was born in Chicago, IL, and holds a B.F.A. from the University of Utah as well as an associate degree from the Imperial Society of Ballroom Dance.  In 1984, Marceau co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine.  Together, Marceau and Dulaine won numerous ballroom dance contests and were four-time winners of the British Exhibition ballroom competition.  Marceau is the recipient of various awards, including the Astaire Award and the Dance Magazine Award.  Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom.  Marceau has been on the faculty at The Juilliard School since 1993 and has taught at the School of American Ballet as well as for numerous New York City social groups, including the Union Club and cotillion societies.  Marceau also currently teaches at the NYU Tisch New Studio on Broadway.

Mad Hot Ballroom | Mad Hot Ballroom is a 2005 American documentary film directed and co-produced by Marilyn Agrelo and written and co-produced by Amy Sewell, about a ballroom dance program in the New York City Department of Education, the New York City public school system for fifth graders.  Several styles of dance are shown in the film, such as tango, foxtrot, swing, rumba and merengue.

Pierre Dulaine | Pierre Dulaine (born 23 April 1944) is a dance instructor and dancer.  He invented the Dulaine method of teaching dance.  He also founded Dancing Classrooms, a Social and Emotional development program for 5th grade children that uses ballroom dancing as a vehicle to change the lives of the children and their families.

Champion Ballroom Academy | The Champion Ballroom Academy (founded April 1990) is a dance studio in San Diego, California.  The studio's main specialties are social partner-dancing, competitive ballroom dance (aka.  Dancesport) and the Latin-dance-based aerobic program called Core Rhythms.

Warren Bullock | Warren Bullock is a professional ballroom dancer and dance teacher.  He owns a dance teaching business with a chain of 14 'studios', and also teaches on cruises.  He has won a number of awards as a dance teacher and coach and together with his wife Jane, pioneered the teaching of Ballroom and Latin American dancing in schools starting at Glebefields School in Tipton and King Edwards School for girls in Edgebaston before speaking and promoting this concept at the BDF conference in 1997 which resulted in dance becoming part of the national curriculum in some counties.  He was the main character in 8 one hour programmes of Baby Ballroom on channel 5Star - a reality documentary made by Firecracker Films based on Bullock, his family and Zig Zag Dance Factory <ref>http://www.channel5.com/show/baby-ballroom/ <ref>http://www.firecrackerfilms.com/broadcast/baby-ballroom/

Ballroom Bootcamp | Ballroom Bootcamp was an American reality television series that debuted on the TLC cable network, on October 7, 2005.  The series follows several individuals who try to improve their skill of dance.  The series ended on January 26, 2007, after two seasons.

Louis Van Amstel | Louis van Amstel (born June 23, 1972) is a Dutch-American ballroom dance champion, professional dancer, and choreographer who appears on the U.S. reality television series "Dancing with the Stars".  He is the creator of the popular dance fitness program LaBlast.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Yvonne Marceau — [1] — "Marceau co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine."
- Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program — [1] — "co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program"
- Pierre Dulaine — [3] — "Pierre Dulaine (born 23 April 1944)"
- Pierre Dulaine — [3] — "He also founded Dancing Classrooms"
- Mad Hot Ballroom (2005 documentary) — [2] — "Mad Hot Ballroom is a 2005 American documentary film"
- Mad Hot Ballroom (featured) — [1] — "which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom."

Ambiguities:
- None identified; "Pierre Dulaine" is consistently named and matched across passages [1] and [3].

Bridge / missing fact:
- None missing. Passages explicitly state that, along with Marceau, Pierre Dulaine co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program. Final answer: Pierre Dulaine.

Retrieval direction:
- Page: "Pierre Dulaine"
- Page: "Yvonne Marceau"
- Page: "Mad Hot Ballroom"
(Verification retrievals; no deferral needed.)
